ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for chocolate milkshake; A0EKL (milk-based flavoured beverages) is the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate milkshake as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Chocolate Milkshake is approximately 500 g (one large cup), as commonly served at food delivery outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shakes, thick, chocolate (SR Legacy, FDC ID 170893)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for chocolate thick shake (FDC ID 170893) used as the primary nutrient anchor. This is an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data. Input energy (110 kcal/100g), protein (2.4g), fat (4.0g), carbohydrate (16.0g), sugar (14.0g), sodium (40mg), dietary fibre (0.6g), calcium (70mg), iron (0.4mg), cholesterol (8mg), saturated fat (2.4g), and trans fat (0.1g) are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for a chocolate milkshake (whole milk, chocolate syrup base). Input values retained with USDA cross-validation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrients not reported in input are set to -1 where USDA data is not directly mappable to this specific commercial preparation. GrabFood was not used as a nutrient source; it is a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 43 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate milkshake (whole milk base), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI approximately 43 for chocolate milk and milkshake preparations. The University of Sydney GI Database is an approved GI-specific source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (43 × 80.0) / 100 = 34.4, where carbohydrate per 500g serving = 16.0 × 5 = 80.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (14g/100g) in a beverage triggers the AHA beverage sugar gate; added sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage (14g sugar/100g); request an unsweetened or sugar-free cocoa milk alternative, and note that even with low sodium (40mg/100g), the high added sugar load independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (14g/100g) in liquid form causes rapid glucose absorption with minimal glycaemic buffering; dietary fibre is only 0.6g/100g, providing negligible glycaemic modulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely due to its high liquid sugar load (14g/100g); if a chocolate milk drink is desired, request an unsweetened cocoa preparation with whole milk to retain protein benefit while eliminating the rapid glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.4g/100g and trans fat at 0.1g/100g contribute to LDL-raising lipid load; high sugar (14g/100g) further promotes triglyceride elevation and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this milkshake due to its combined saturated fat (2.4g/100g), trans fat (0.1g/100g), and high added sugar (14g/100g), all of which contribute to adverse lipid profiles and cardiovascular risk; an unsweetened, low-fat cocoa milk would be a more suitable alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (14g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; saturated fat further drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as its high sugar and saturated fat content promote systemic inflammation without any offsetting omega-3, polyphenol, or meaningful fibre benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Milkshake (巧克力奶昔; Malay: Minuman Susu Coklat) is a cold blended beverage made from whole milk, chocolate syrup, chocolate bits, and sugar, yielding a rich, velvety, and intensely sweet drink. It is commonly served in a large cup (approximately 500g per serving) at cafes, fast-food outlets, and food delivery platforms in Singapore. The texture is thick and creamy due to the emulsification of milk fat and chocolate, and the flavour profile is dominated by sweet cocoa with a milky finish. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 110 kcal, 2.4g protein, 4.0g fat (2.4g saturated), 16.0g carbohydrate, and 14.0g sugar, with 40mg sodium and 70mg calcium. The high sugar content (14g/100g in liquid form) is a primary clinical concern: liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing significant postprandial glucose spikes with minimal glycaemic buffering from the low dietary fibre (0.6g/100g). This beverage is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high added sugar load, and carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to its saturated and trans fat content. The glycemic index is estimated at 43 (low GI category) per the University of Sydney GI Database, but the large serving size (500g) yields a high glycemic load of approximately 34.4 per serving, making it clinically significant for glycaemic management. Calcium content (70mg/100g) provides some nutritional benefit from the milk base. Nutrient values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170893).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate milkshake?
Is chocolate milkshake high in sugar?
What is the GI of chocolate milkshake?
Is chocolate milkshake suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary nutrient source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170893) for chocolate thick shake and found broadly consistent. Confidence is set to medium because the exact commercial formulation of the Choco Velvet Milkshake is not available in any approved primary source; USDA SR Legacy provides the closest defensible anchor.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific commercial chocolate milkshake preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values were not reported in the input record and are not directly mappable from USDA SR Legacy to this specific commercial preparation without risk of misattribution. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a chocolate milk-based beverage per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no vitamin C is contributed by milk, chocolate syrup, or chocolate bits in standard formulations. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.1: retained from input; consistent with trace trans fat from dairy fat in whole milk, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to a valid HPB Foodo nutrient database entry and was not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170893) was used as the approved nutrient anchor instead. GrabFood is a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2 and was not used for nutrient evidence. 6. Cultural tags in input (Chinese, Milk Tea, Tea) are not accurate for a chocolate milkshake; cuisine reclassified as International/Singaporean. 7. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for chocolate milkshake per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); GL per 500g serving = 34.4.
